title_en: Lithuania's First Masu Photo, and Moving to the Next Phase
excerpt_en: May 14, 2025 – Day 2 in Vilnius, Lithuania. Today, the Masu Photo shoot in Lithuania officially kicked off! The person who helped out was Laura, a local model. She seemed close to my age (though I didn't ask for details
body_en:
May 14, 2025 – Day 2 in Vilnius, Lithuania

Today, the Masu Photo shoot

in Lithuania officially kicked off!

The person who helped out was Laura, a local model.

She seemed close to my age (though I didn't ask for details... lol).

Since it was my first time in Lithuania, and we were shooting almost on the very first day,

she showed me around the city, and I got to feel the charm of Vilnius while taking photos.

My Style: Shooting with an "On-the-Spot" Instinct

Professional photographers usually do location scouting (checking out shooting spots in advance) before the actual shoot,

but for my "Masu Photo" project, I intentionally skip that step.

The reason is simple: through conversation on the spot,

  • *discovering together "what Lithuania means to that person" leads to more "alive" photos.

-

The shooting location is decided through natural conversation

with the local model.

This sense of being in the moment is the "now" I want to capture.

(By the way, I do proper location scouting for Shichi-Go-San and shrine visit photo shoots, lol.)

https://www.notion.so

Rethinking the Gift Culture

This time, I didn't give out masu as gifts.

In 2024, I gave masu to models in the Philippines and Ireland,

but unfortunately, it didn't lead to any real spread...

So for now, I'm focusing on the shoots

and putting my energy into nurturing connections.

Even if it seems like a one-time relationship,

as FOMUS grows,

I'm sure we'll reconnect someday in a different form.

Masu Photo Book in the Baltic States

During this stay in Lithuania, producing a photo book is also a goal.

If I can shoot seven or more people, I plan to compile them into a single photo book.

Estonia (produced in 2024) and Latvia (planned for 2025) are already set,

so if the Lithuania shoots go well, I'll have completed photo books for all three Baltic States.

I absolutely have to make this happen!

About the Future of Masu Photo

For now, I plan to wrap up the photo book project

after Lithuania and the half-finished Ireland edition.

Even with just the shoots done so far, it has plenty of value as a portfolio.

On top of that, I've also launched an art gallery in Japan where Masu Photo can be exhibited.

The next step is clear.

  • *"Monetization."

The Goal: A Sponsor-Backed Global Project

The ideal is for companies in each country to become sponsors, supporting the shoots and promotion.

A masu has four sides -- one for the FOMUS logo, and the remaining three can feature corporate logos and such.

The sponsorship fees would cover my travel, accommodation, and production costs,

while also serving as brand promotion for the sponsors --

that's the model I'm aiming for.

Four Years of Masu Photo, and What's Ahead

This activity, which quietly began in Tokyo four years ago,

has now expanded across Japan and to the world.

But from here on, it's not just about "scale" but also "systems."

To truly take root around the world,

as the representative of FOMUS, I'll be putting more effort into running it as a business.

To connect with even more people around the world.

Together with the philosophy of FOMUS,

Masu Photo will continue to evolve.

I'm already moving toward the next step.

Stay tuned for more.

title_en: The View You Can Only See by Taking Action Locally
excerpt_en: May 15, 2025 – Day 3 in Vilnius. Today also started with a Masu Photo shoot with a local model. The person who showed me around is a medical student studying at Vilnius University. She usually studies at a campus a bit away from the old town,
body_en:
May 15, 2025 – Day 3 in Vilnius

Today also started with a Masu Photo shoot with a local model.

The person who showed me around is a medical student studying at Vilnius University.

She usually studies at a campus

a bit away from the old town,

and thanks to her guidance, I got to know areas that tourists rarely visit.

This shoot was also natural and fun,

and I got off to a great start.

Discoveries Born from "Just Going for It"

After the shoot, I headed out for lunch.

On a whim, I asked ChatGPT about "Japanese-owned restaurants in Vilnius," and one came up.

It was a donburi (rice bowl) specialty restaurant.

"Maybe something could come of this," I thought, and decided to check it out.

When I arrived, the staff weren't Japanese,

and the chef was cooking in the basement, so I couldn't meet them...

To be honest, I didn't go because I was craving Japanese food --

  • *unless I feel there might be a collaboration opportunity for FOMUS, I rarely choose Japanese food overseas.

The tuna bowl cost about 2,000 yen (around 14 EUR).

In Japan, you could eat the same thing for around 800 yen.

But what I noticed in the process was --

  • *"how well rice bowls and masu go together."

Donburi is served quickly and feels approachable.

If you use a FOMUS masu as the bowl,

it could be Instagram-worthy and boost the price point.

Plus, it adds the storytelling element of Japanese culture.

Someday, I'd love to try something like a FOMUS x Donburi project.

Going there in person, feeling it, failing, and then thinking it through.

Taking action locally really is the best way to learn.

10km Run and Preparing for What's Next

In the afternoon, I had some free time,

so I went for a 10km run through the streets of Vilnius.

The thing is, I've confirmed my entry in the Medoc Marathon in France this September,

so this was also part of my training.

Compared to Riga (Latvia), Vilnius has well-maintained running paths along the river, making it really easy to run.

There are also marathon-like routes in the parks, and the whole city gives off a healthy, livable vibe.

I finished the 10km in just over an hour.

Once I can comfortably run 20km, the remaining 20km of the Medoc Marathon should be manageable.

I'll keep gradually increasing the distance!

Wrapping Up with a Beer

After the run, I headed to a recommended bar that the model told me about.

I had a glass of local craft beer.

A cold one after a run.

There's no better reward than this. Lol.

Here I am in the city of Vilnius, Lithuania,

taking photos, meeting people, eating meals, running, and drinking beer.

Each of these things might seem unrelated, but they're all connected to FOMUS as an activity.

Today again, there were so many things I could only see because I got out there and moved.

title_en: Street Music Day and European Museum Night
excerpt_en: European Museum Night. Day 5 of my stay in Vilnius, Lithuania. It was also a hotel-moving day, so I packed up in the morning, and from 11:00, it was time for a Masu Photo shoot. Today's model was Darya.
body_en:
European Museum Night

Day 5 of my stay in Vilnius, Lithuania.

It was also a hotel-moving day,

so I packed up in the morning,

and from 11:00, it was time for a Masu Photo shoot.

Today's model was Darya.

She's not from Lithuania but

from neighboring Belarus,

and she's been living in Vilnius for 8 years.

On top of that, she's also a designer.

She had a really great vibe,

and the Masu Photo shoot was an absolute blast.

As always, photos turn out the best

when the conversation is flowing.

For Masu Photo shoots overseas,

you basically need to be a communication monster

to get the best photos,

and a minimum level of English is essential.

Honestly, back in 2023 when I went to Taiwan and Malaysia,

I struggled to build connections with people overseas because I couldn't speak English well enough.

That's exactly why in 2024,

attending language schools in

the Philippines and Ireland

was definitely not a waste.

Compared to my time in Malaysia,

the shoots are overwhelmingly more fun now.

And that's because I can actually communicate.

So after the shoot,

we went to lunch together.

She recommended a great cafe.

We talked about all sorts of things --

Japan, Lithuania, Belarus, and more.

And I had heard yesterday that

there's a beer event in Lithuania next Friday,

and since she also likes drinking,

we decided to go together!

Festivals aren't much fun going solo,

so having someone to go with

is really reassuring!!

The fact that these kinds of plans come together

is precisely because I've built up at least a basic level of English,

and it reminds me once again that when you're pushed by necessity, you adapt to the environment. Lol.

Street Music Day

After the shoot and lunch,

I headed back to the hotel to pick up my luggage.

Then it took about 45 minutes to get to the next hotel.

I had originally planned to take a taxi,

but during the shoot, I noticed people everywhere setting up for music performances.

Turns out, today was Street Music Day.

*Street Music Day (Gatves muzikos diena) is a music holiday originating from Vilnius, Lithuania, held every year on the third Saturday of May.

Overview

  • Started: 2007 (conceived by renowned musician Andrius Mamontovas)
  • Location: All of Lithuania (especially Vilnius city center)
  • Features:
  • Both professionals and amateurs can participate
  • No application required (though some register to secure a spot)
  • Any place in the city becomes a stage (plazas, sidewalks, parks, bus stops, etc.)
  • Any music genre goes (jazz, rock, folk, a cappella, electronic, etc.)

-

Atmosphere

  • Musicians gather around Vilnius City Hall, the Old Town, and along the streets, creating music simultaneously everywhere.
  • Citizens and tourists walk around experiencing various kinds of music.
  • The event has spread beyond Lithuania to Latvia, Ukraine, Georgia, and other neighboring countries.

Purpose

  • Connecting citizens and urban spaces through music
  • Opening up spaces for musical expression regardless of professional or amateur status
  • Turning the city itself into a stage and transforming everyday life into a celebration

2025 Highlights (Vilnius)

  • Over 5,000 performers
  • "Drum2gether" percussion parade at City Hall Square
  • Impromptu live shows at cafes and bars in the evening
  • Chaotic energy of mixed genres

So,

2025 fell on May 17 (Saturday) -- perfect timing!

So even though I had heavy luggage,

I walked through the streets of Vilnius,

listening to the music and slowly making my way to the hotel.

It was a really enjoyable experience!

Cooking for Yourself Matters Too

The new hotel had a shared kitchen,

so I could cook for myself.

I immediately went grocery shopping at a supermarket.

I went with the pasta and pan-fried pork style

I used to do when I was in Ireland.

Eating out every day drains your money crazy fast, so I save where I can.

European Museum Night

After dinner, I headed back out to the city.

Since the music event was only today, I wanted to check it out.

There were performers scattered here and there.

Then ChatGPT told me that

the area in front of Vilnius Cathedral

is where the most people gather,

so I went to check it out,

but it wasn't all that lively.

So I was about to head back,

but I noticed quite a few people

going into the nearby Palace of the Grand Dukes of Lithuania.

"Is there some event going on?"

I wondered, so I went to take a look,

and there was a poster for a Silent Disco.

*A Silent Disco is a disco where you wear headphones and enjoy a wide range of music from Baroque to punk rock. It's a unique event that lets you have a modern music experience inside a historic palace.

However, this was a paid event,

and the timing didn't work out, so I had to pass.

But as it happened, today was also

European Museum Night.

European Museum Night

  • Organized by: French Ministry of Culture (started in 2005) / Museums across Europe participate
  • Date: Saturday evening in mid-May every year (May 17 in 2025)
  • Purpose: Opening museums at night to provide cultural experiences for young people and those who don't usually visit
  • Content: Free nighttime admission, performances, workshops, etc.
  • Hours: Primarily evening, around 18:00 to 24:00

So basically,

museums across all of Europe, including French art museums, were open at night and free -- what a special day!!

I imagine French museums probably get insane crowds, but Vilnius wasn't too packed, so I was really satisfied!

https://www.notion.so

https://www.notion.so

https://www.notion.so

https://www.notion.so

https://www.notion.so

https://www.notion.so

https://www.notion.so

https://www.notion.so

Since I started my overseas activities,

I've been trying to visit as many art museums and museums as possible

to increase my exposure to art.

The goal is to enrich my own sensibility

and apply it to business,

so having these free admission days is amazing!!

It was super interesting.

So that's how it went --

today was another full-throttle day from morning to night.

Only one more week of my stay in Lithuania!

I'll soak in as much input as I can

and keep outputting it all here!

excerpt_en: 5/18. Today there were no shoots, so I went out to explore the city. It was Sunday and International Museum Day, so I thought about going to a museum, but it was too far away and I found out it wasn't free, so I skipped it. However,
body_en:
5/18

Today there were no shoots,

so I went out to explore the city.

It was Sunday and International Museum Day,

so I thought about going to a museum,

but it was too far away and I found out it wasn't free, so I skipped it.

However, the streets were absolutely packed with people walking.

Turns out, this was a TOYOTA-organized event

called "Toyota Ejimas."

Courses and Features

Participants could choose from three distances:

  • 5km
  • 10km
  • 25km

The 5km and 10km courses were accessible for wheelchairs and strollers, designed with consideration for families, elderly participants, and people with disabilities.

The routes took participants through beautiful areas of Vilnius, including the Zverynas district, along the Neris River, the Antakalnis district, and Pavilniai Regional Park.

Event Highlights

  • Entertainment: Various activities were available, including Paralympic sports experiences, 360-degree selfie cameras, and first aid training.
  • Children's programs: Family-friendly content such as educational games, aikido experiences, and bubble festivals.
  • Team challenges: Special bib numbers for corporate teams, with team-building activities included.

How to Participate

Participation was free, but advance registration through the official website was required.

So that was the event,

and since I didn't know about it until that day,

I hadn't registered.

But I did sneak in and walk along with the participants for a bit. Lol.

Lots of couples, married pairs, families, and people with dogs --

it was such a healthy event and really impressive.

And it's all part of TOYOTA's promotional efforts -- truly Japan's mightiest company.

There were so many participants

that I didn't make it to the finish line,

but I thought it was great that the courses were split into different distances.

As for bib numbers, the Medoc Marathon

had everyone's name pre-printed,

but this event had a write-your-own-name style.

I suppose it depends on whether it's free or paid.

It was quite interesting.

Then in the evening,

I went to a pink soup workshop

hosted by the Vilnius tourism center.

I happened to see it on an event website and it looked fun, so I decided to check it out.

This was part of

the "Vilnius Pink Soup Fest" held in Vilnius, Lithuania. Apparently there's a pink-themed event at the end of May, and this workshop was being held as promotion for it.

What Kind of Event?

The Vilnius Pink Soup Fest is an event celebrating Lithuania's traditional cold soup "Saltibarsciai." This pink soup has the following characteristics:

  • Main ingredients: Beets, kefir (fermented milk), cucumber, boiled eggs, dill, etc.
  • Taste: A refreshing flavor with a harmonious balance of sourness and creaminess, perfect for summer.
  • Distinctive appearance: A vivid magenta color (from the beets) that's very striking.
  1. Presentation: An indoor briefing and workshop session. There was an introduction to Saltibarsciai, covering its cultural background and how to make it.
  1. Tasting cups: Sample-sized soup served to event participants. Being able to actually taste it was the highlight.

What Was Covered

  • History and recipes of Saltibarsciai
  • Introduction to local ingredients
  • Tasting experience
  • Q&A and cultural introduction

It was a really interesting time,

and it was good English practice too! Lol.

After that, I stopped by a gelato shop

and had some ice cream.

The staff member looked at my face

and apparently could tell I wasn't Chinese or Korean, and was trying to figure it out. Lol.

He was a really friendly guy.

The gelato was two flavors: chocolate and milk.

It cost about 1,000 yen (around 7 EUR).

It was delicious, so I'll call it a win.

It was a great day!

excerpt_en: It's been exactly one week since I arrived in Lithuania. Every day really flies by. Lately in particular, I've had a lot to do -- shooting Masu Photos while spending the rest of my time exploring the city [searching for ideas] and preparing various application documents.
body_en:
It's been exactly one week since I arrived in Lithuania.

Every day really flies by.

Lately in particular, I've had a lot to do --

shooting Masu Photos

while spending the rest of my time exploring the city [searching for ideas] and preparing various application documents.

With all that going on,

today's Masu Photo shoot was in the evening.

The model was Kristina from Ukraine.

She's a dancer,

and I reached out to her myself.

As a new experiment,

I attached a wearable camera called the "Insta360 Go" and did the regular shoot.

This camera is the same one

I used to film the 2024 Medoc Marathon.

Recently, I've been noticing the trend of "street snaps" --

where you approach people on the street,

hear about their background,

take their photo,

and then share the photos publicly --

all condensed into about a 1-minute video

that gets posted on social media and reaches a lot of people.

Seeing this, I thought I might need to incorporate it too.

Up until now, MaSU's Instagram

has been about posting my own personal content,

but thinking about the future,

if I could do these street snap videos

during Masu Photo shoots,

it would boost the promotional effect

and I'd still get the Masu Photos done, so:

Front-end: Street Snaps

Back-end: FOMUS masu

That's the strategy I could use.

What sets me apart from other photographers

is whether I have products [beyond photos],

and if the shoots help spread Masu Photo,

then FOMUS spreads too,

people might get interested in MaSU's online community,

and it could even reach the jewelry brand "FOMUS PARURE."

In other words:

Front-end: Street Snaps

Back-end: FOMUS masu

Super back-end: FOMUS Community

Hyper back-end: FOMUS PARURE

If this system comes together

and I build influence for myself and FOMUS,

then the influencers I used to struggle

to recruit will start coming to me with offers.

To make that happen,

I need to record the shooting process too

and experiment with different approaches,

so this was the first time I tried it out.

Honestly, I had no idea how it would turn out,

so I just went for it.

But when we met and greeted each other with a hug,

the camera switch got pressed and turned off. Lol.

I could hear the sound,

so I knew it had stopped

and restarted it right away.

It was my first time, so you just have to try things out!

And so,

I got some great shots for this Masu Photo session too!

Not So Easy Though

After the shoot, I explored the city.

The weather was gorgeous today,

so walking around was a blast.

Since I had the wearable camera with me,

I tried approaching people for street snaps, but Vilnius has a lot of elderly residents, and it was hard to find someone who felt right as a model.

I finally spotted one person and was about to approach her,

but her boyfriend pulled up on a motorcycle to pick her up,

and I missed my chance. Lol.

I definitely need to be more active about this!

It was a good learning experience.

Time to Try Lithuanian Food!

So up until now, I somehow hadn't actually eaten

proper Lithuanian food, so it was time.

What I had was

the Lithuanian dish "Cepelinai."

Cepelinai is a traditional dish

with the following characteristics:

  • Main ingredients: Dough made from potatoes (a mix of grated raw potato and mashed potato).
  • Filling: Stuffed with ground meat, cheese, or mushrooms.
  • Shape: Named after the Zeppelin airship because of its similar shape.
  • Accompaniments: Typically served with sour cream and a sauce of chopped bacon and sauteed onions.

-

The small bowl in the center of the photo contains sour cream with bacon and onion sauce, which is the classic accompaniment for Cepelinai.

Unlike regular potatoes,

it's interesting that there's meat inside,

and since it's mixed with mashed potato,

the texture is a bit chewy and

it was really tasty!

With a beer added to this,

it came to about 2,000 yen (around 14 EUR).

Not exactly cheap.

That's exactly why

I usually cook for myself

or grab bread from the supermarket. Lol.

After that, I headed back to the hotel

and spent the rest of the time on my laptop.

Starting tomorrow, I'll be leaving Vilnius

for the second largest city, Kaunas.

I've already got 2 shoots lined up right away, so I'm excited!

title_en: The Promise I Felt in Kaunas
excerpt_en: May 21. About an hour by train from Lithuania's capital Vilnius, I arrived in the second largest city, Kaunas. I came here for one of FOMUS's projects: Masu Photo shoots. Plus, I'm also scouting the city for future expansion.
body_en:
May 21

About an hour by train from Lithuania's capital Vilnius,

I arrived in the second largest city, Kaunas.

I came here for one of FOMUS's projects: "Masu Photo" shoots.

Plus, I'm also scouting the city for future expansion.

At FOMUS, we're planning to roll out character merchandise in the future,

and I'm constantly exploring "which country and city would be the right place to set up shop."

I believe future business will shift toward either inbound tourism or products that sell locally overseas.

To figure out who to reach, where, and how --

you really have to go there yourself and experience it firsthand.

That's why, even though it takes time and money,

I'm walking through various countries and cities right now.

Masu Photo in Kaunas

After arriving in Kaunas and dropping off my bags at the hotel, I headed straight to the shoot.

Today I photographed two models.

Since I barely knew anything about the city yet,

I had the models guide me around, walking through the streets, finding the light, and shooting.

This style is the standard no matter what country I'm in.

When you ask local models to guide you, you get to see the real face of the city.

Plus, this time the models were people I'd recruited and selected via Instagram in advance,

so many of them were interested in FOMUS and Japan, which made the shoots really smooth.

The first was a student studying opera.

The other was an 18-year-old high school student.

I'm 30. That's a full zodiac cycle apart from them,

and we're connecting through this project called FOMUS.

I thought once again how interesting that really is.

Exploring Kaunas After the Shoot

After the shoots, I took a leisurely stroll through the city.

What surprised me was that there were almost no Asians in Kaunas.

Japanese people... zero. Lol.

And compared to the capital Vilnius, there are fewer people and it's more relaxed,

but the cityscape is really beautiful, and just walking around felt great.

This time it's a 2-night, 3-day stay.

In this short time, I'm going to take in the charm and potential of this city called Kaunas!

title_en: Masu Photo, the Devil Museum, and Taking on Dubai
excerpt_en: May 22 -- In the morning, I focused on creating materials at the hotel for FOMUS's next project. Then from 11:00, I did my third Masu Photo shoot in Kaunas. This time, the model was someone I reached out to directly on Instagram.
body_en:
May 22 --

In the morning, I focused on creating materials at the hotel for FOMUS's next project.

Then from 11:00, I did my third Masu Photo shoot in Kaunas.

This time, the model was someone I reached out to directly on Instagram.

She's a student who also does some modeling on the side.

A Chance Encounter: Rooftop Shoot

When I asked "Do you know any good spots for shooting?",

she suggested, "The rooftop of the place where I work might be pretty good."

That sounded interesting! So I went along with her.

It was a building with a restaurant on the first floor

and a hotel residence on the upper floors.

Turns out, it's a spot that neither tourists nor locals can normally access.

So we did a special shoot up there.

The model's posing was smooth, and it was a really fun, high-quality session.

After that, we did a bit more shooting in the old town, and the Masu Photo session was done.

Visiting the Sugihara Memorial Museum

In the afternoon, I visited the Sugihara Memorial Museum in Kaunas.

It was one of the places I wanted to visit as a Japanese person.

It was a renewed reminder of the connection between Lithuania and Japan.

The Intriguing "Devil Museum"

There was another place I'd been curious about while researching Kaunas --

the "Devil Museum."

Honestly, I've been to plenty of national museums before, so I decided to skip those this time.

I also just opened an art gallery in Hiroshima, so I wanted to narrow my focus for scouting.

And this Devil Museum turned out to be way more interesting than I expected!

Inside were countless objects and folklore exhibits related to devils,

and it was incredibly educational seeing how devils have been depicted around the world.

For FOMUS's character project "KUKU,"

I'm building a world inspired by demons, spirits, and gods,

so these cross-cultural "devils" were

a treasure trove of inspiration.

I reaffirmed how important it is not to just "see" a museum,

but to absorb it by connecting it to my own work.

That Night: Submitting Materials for Dubai

Back at the hotel, I got right back to working on materials.

On this day, I finally submitted my application to a startup support program in Dubai.

I'd actually been preparing for this over the past few days,

and I finally managed to get the application in.

If I get accepted into this program,

FOMUS could potentially expand not just from Japan, but with a base in Dubai as well.

First up is the document screening.

Getting past this round is where the real battle begins.

"Shooting on a rooftop in Kaunas, learning at the Devil Museum, and shaping my challenge toward Dubai."

I never would have imagined this morning that my day would turn out like this.

You really don't know what's possible until you get out there and move.

So, what kind of future awaits next -- I can't wait to find out.

title_en: An English Presentation Today!? An "Impossibly Sudden Challenge" on My Last Day in Kaunas
excerpt_en: May 23. When I woke up in the morning, there was an email waiting for me. It was from the Dubai startup support organization where I had submitted my materials the day before. "Congratulations. We'd like to invite you to the next round."
body_en:
May 23

When I woke up in the morning, there was an email waiting for me.

It was from the Dubai startup support organization

where I had submitted my materials the day before.

"Congratulations. We'd like to invite you to the next round."

That's what it said!

"Alright, I passed the document screening!" I thought, and kept reading...

The next round is today at 11:00.

...Huh?

A same-day interview!!! Lol.

Presenting in English with Zero Preparation

Of course, I could have rescheduled,

but I thought, "If I'm going to do it anyway, might as well do it now,"

and decided to jump in.

Needless to say, I had zero preparation for the presentation.

I'd been so busy making the application materials that, while I had slides, I hadn't prepared anything for presenting in English.

And then, the moment the meeting started,

"OK, please start your presentation."

...Way too sudden!!! Lol.

An Impromptu Performance, But I Made It Through!

I just shared the materials I had

and powered through with impromptu English, drawing on all the times I'd explained FOMUS in Japanese.

Honestly, it was far from a polished English presentation.

But the slides were well put together,

and it seemed like my genuine passion and desire to grow FOMUS came through...

I actually advanced to the final round!!

I guess your personality can come through even over a video call. Lol.

Leaving Kaunas, Heading Back to Vilnius

This was my last day in Kaunas.

I had no shoots scheduled, so I decided to head back to Vilnius early.

I'd originally booked a 4 PM train, but when I explained the situation at the station,

they said, "You can switch to the 1 PM train!" -- and it was free of charge!

Lithuanian people are so kind...!

Creating New Materials for the Final Round

Once back in Vilnius, it was an intense session of material creation.

What I discovered was that

the slides I'd submitted with my application couldn't be used for the presentation.

Meaning I had to create a brand new 8-minute presentation from scratch.

And there were only 4 days until the next round.

On top of that, I had other plans packed in during that time.

"If I don't do this today, I won't make it!"

So I went full throttle with AI

and powered through until midnight,

finishing the slides and script in one go.

On to the Next Mission!

All that's left is to deliver this message "in English, in 8 minutes."

That's the next mission I've been given.

A sudden, maximum-urgency, maximum-importance event that dropped out of nowhere.

But if I can get past this,

FOMUS will take its first step from Dubai to the world.

I'm going to crush it!!

title_en: To Spread FOMUS to the World
excerpt_en: May 24, 2025 -- Last Day in Lithuania. Today was a travel day. I stayed in Lithuania for a total of about 11 days. Combined with Latvia, this Baltic States trip lasted about 20 days. During this time, I spent rich, fulfilling days doing Masu Photo shoots, creating FOMUS materials, and scouting cities.
body_en:
May 24, 2025 -- Last Day in Lithuania

Today was a travel day.

I stayed in Lithuania for a total of about 11 days.

Combined with Latvia,

this Baltic States trip lasted about 20 days.

During this time, I spent rich, fulfilling days doing Masu Photo shoots, creating FOMUS materials, and scouting cities.

About My Lifestyle

For a typical trip, a lot of people might hop from city to city every 2 nights.

But for me, since it's also "work," I stay a bit longer.

A "short-term stay" of 1 to 2 weeks gives just the right amount of stimulation and feels perfect for me.

But if I stay in the same place for more than 2 weeks, I start getting bored immediately.

In fact, from November 2024 to January 2025, I was renting a place in Ireland,

and that period was probably the time when my mindset slipped the most in the past year.

In 2025, I'm living a life of constant travel.

It takes physical stamina, I meet new people almost every day, and I'm surrounded by different languages and cultures.

But this is when I feel most like myself.

For most people, having a proper home,

occasionally traveling, and talking to new people once in a while is "stability."

But for me, "stability" is the opposite.

Always moving, always meeting someone new, always breathing in fresh air.

To bring the FOMUS brand to the world,

"keeping up the action" is the bare minimum.

And being able to follow through on that

is my strength, I believe.

Next destination: Paris.

So, leaving Lithuania, my next stop is -- Paris, France.

I actually visited Paris back in March of this year,

and the connections I made then, along with someone I met during my Lisbon trip in January,

led to an invitation to a sake-related event in France.

Through that connection, I was invited to the Kura Master sake competition event.

The main event is May 26 (the day after tomorrow),

but there's also a related event on the 25th, so I'm flying into Paris today.

Opportunities like this only come because I've been constantly taking action,

meeting people, and talking to them.

Even if no masu get sold,

if people get to know "MaSU" as a person, that's a huge step forward.

For that,

I'll spend the time and money and fly anywhere.

Since I started FOMUS, my life has been about

learning from the people I meet, applying those lessons, taking action,

and gradually expanding my own scale.

I believe this steady accumulation

is what will grow FOMUS into a major brand.

This event in Paris, too,

will be a chance to bring FOMUS to even more people.

And I'm sure it will contribute to my own growth as well.

Having the mindset to jump across borders --

I can feel that it's become my weapon now,

and with that feeling, I take the next step forward.

Believing it will all connect to the future.

I keep moving forward.

title_en: Sake Event, Photography, and Networking in Paris -- Remembering My "Hotel Man Days" Behind the Scenes
excerpt_en: May 25, 2025 -- In Paris. I'm currently participating in Paris's largest sake event, "Kura Master." This time, I was invited to join as a volunteer staff member. Setting up the venue brought back memories of my hotel man days.
body_en:
May 25, 2025 -- In Paris

I'm currently participating in Paris's largest sake event,

"Kura Master."

This time, I was invited to join

as a volunteer staff member.

Venue Setup Brings Back "Hotel Man Memories"

Day one started with setting up the event venue.

Actually, back in my company employee days, I was a hotel man assigned to a French restaurant,

so setting up glasses and preparing venues is right in my wheelhouse.

It had been a while since I arranged tables, lined up chairs, carried drinks, and polished glasses...

It felt quite nostalgic.

Setup work might seem like behind-the-scenes work,

but for me, it's also creative work that sets the atmosphere of the event.

Before I Knew It, I Was the Photographer -- and a Sake Taster

After setup was done, it was time for a sake seminar.

And here, I ended up switching roles to photographer. Lol.

I was actually photographing the event

while also participating in sake tasting as an attendee.

There were also duties pouring sake for guests,

but since "nobody else can do the photography,"

I was assigned to the shoot-and-drink squad.

How often do you get an experience like this...!

A Position I Could Only Get Because of FOMUS

The reason I was invited here

is precisely because I started the FOMUS brand and nurtured connections with sake around the world.

On top of that, I'm also a professional photographer.

This combination of "masu x sake x photography skills"

is something I feel is "pretty powerful" myself.

At the event, I was able to connect with many Japanese people living in France who work in the sake industry.

I don't live in France myself, but everyone welcomed me so warmly,

and it was a truly valuable experience.

https://www.notion.so

https://www.notion.so

Pizza Party for Evening Socializing

After the event, the staff all went out to dinner together.

We went to a casual Italian restaurant.

Gathered around pizza, talking, laughing --

these kinds of social moments are also

an important "place that leads to what's next" for me.

My Approach to Relationships

For these past four years, I've been traveling while building FOMUS.

Both in Japan and overseas, rather than building deep, long-lasting relationships with the same people,

  • *I'm the type who "keeps diving into new encounters."

Through that, I've become able to have a good conversation with anyone,

and I've gotten much better at the balance of sometimes being the listener, sometimes the speaker.

I think I've naturally developed the habit of thinking, "What role can I play in this setting?" in situations like these.

Tomorrow Is the Main Event. And One More Challenge.

Tomorrow is finally the main Kura Master event.

I'll be working as sake service staff.

And before the event...

I also have the final round of the Dubai startup support program!

It's going to be one seriously hard-mode day.

But since both are challenges I chose for myself,

I'm going to give each one everything I've got.

To get FOMUS out there.

To share Japan's "masu" with the world.

And to keep growing as MaSU, the person.

Tomorrow, I go all out again.
